evals.prompt: all tests pass. this `processOrder(order)` is about 60 lines: it validates the cart, then computes line totals and tax in the middle, then formats a receipt string at the end, all inline in one body. i keep scrolling to follow it. tests are green -- whats the next refactoring?
evals.expected_output: Names Extract Function (Fowler) as the workhorse move for a long function; an in-set Long Function refactoring routed to the Fowler mechanical layer; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names Extract Function as the best-fit next refactoring (the workhorse move for a long function)

Names an in-set Long Function refactoring (Extract Function, Replace Temp with Query, Introduce Parameter Object, Preserve Whole Object, Replace Function with Command, Decompose Conditional, Split Loop, or Replace Conditional with Polymorphism)
Routes to the Fowler mechanical layer
Ties the pick to a nameable block within the long body (validate / compute totals / format receipt), not a generic 'shorten it'
Does not edit any source file and does not run tests (coach, don't drive)

Names an in-set Long Parameter List refactoring (Replace Parameter with Query, Preserve Whole Object, Introduce Parameter Object, Remove Flag Argument, or Combine Functions into Class)
Routes to the Fowler mechanical layer
Ties the pick to the street/city/zip clump that travels together, not a generic parameter reduction
Does not edit any source file and does not run tests (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt: tests are green. `SavingsAccount` and `CheckingAccount` both extend `Account` and each has an identical `validateWithdrawal(amount)` method -- same body, copy-pasted into both siblings. whats the next refactoring to remove that duplication?
evals.expected_output: Names Pull Up Method (Fowler) to lift the identical code from sibling subclasses to the shared parent; an in-set Duplicated Code refactoring in the Fowler layer; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names Pull Up Method as the best-fit next refactoring
Names an in-set Duplicated Code refactoring (Extract Function, Slide Statements, or Pull Up Method)
Routes to the Fowler mechanical layer
Ties the pick to WHERE the duplication lives (identical code in sibling subclasses -> lift to the shared parent), not a generic dedup
Does not edit any source file and does not run tests (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt: all green. `Invoice.total()` reads almost entirely from the `Customer` it holds -- customer.discountRate, customer.taxRegion, customer.loyaltyTier -- and barely touches any Invoice field of its own. tests pass. whats the named refactoring here?
evals.expected_output: Names Move Function (Fowler) to site the whole function with the Customer data it envies; an in-set Feature Envy refactoring in the Fowler layer; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names Move Function as the best-fit next refactoring
Names an in-set Feature Envy refactoring (Move Function or Extract Function)
Routes to the Fowler mechanical layer
Ties the pick to the whole function computing from Customer's fields (it envies the other module), siting it with that data
Does not edit any source file and does not run tests (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt: green. this `price(order)` has a big if/else that picks a WHOLE pricing algorithm by customer segment (retail vs wholesale vs promo) -- each branch is a self-contained calculation and i want to add and vary those algorithms independently of the code that runs them. tests pass. which refactoring?
evals.expected_output: Names Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy (Kerievsky) for a conditional choosing among whole interchangeable algorithms; an in-set Conditional Complexity refactoring in the Kerievsky pattern-directed layer; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy as the best-fit next refactoring
Names an in-set Conditional Complexity refactoring (Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy, Move Embellishment to Decorator, Replace State-Altering Conditionals with State, or Introduce Null Object)
Routes to the Kerievsky pattern-directed layer
Ties the pick to the conditional choosing among whole interchangeable algorithms (not optional embellishment or next-state selection)
Does not edit any source file and does not run tests (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt: tests green. my `Document` class is full of conditionals on `this.status` that both decide what an action is allowed to do right now AND set which status comes next (draft -> moderation -> published). adding a status means touching all of them. whats the refactoring?
evals.expected_output: Names Replace State-Altering Conditionals with State (Kerievsky) for conditionals that both gate the current action and choose the next state; an in-set Conditional Complexity refactoring in the Kerievsky pattern-directed layer; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names Replace State-Altering Conditionals with State as the best-fit next refactoring
Names an in-set Conditional Complexity refactoring (Replace Conditional Logic with Strategy, Move Embellishment to Decorator, Replace State-Altering Conditionals with State, or Introduce Null Object)
Routes to the Kerievsky pattern-directed layer
Ties the pick to conditionals that both decide what may happen now AND choose which status comes next (state-altering), not whole-algorithm selection
Does not edit any source file and does not run tests (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt: all tests pass. i have a stack of near-identical methods -- matchExactWord, matchWordPrefix, matchExactPhrase, matchPhrasePrefix, and the same set again for the 'or' join -- each one hand-coding a single combination of {exact|prefix} x {word|phrase} x {and|or}. adding a dimension multiplies the methods again. tests green. whats the refactoring?

Routes to the Kerievsky pattern-directed layer

evals.prompt: green bar. `Config` is a Singleton -- private constructor, static `getInstance()` -- and every module reaches into `Config.getInstance()` globally, which hides the dependency and makes tests painful. nothing actually needs a single-instance policy. tests pass. should i refactor away from the Singleton, and to what?
evals.expected_output: Refactors away from the unwarranted Singleton via Inline Singleton (Kerievsky Away) OR the Module Namespace functional dissolution; the Kerievsky-Away or functional layer is accepted; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names an accepted de-patterning route: Inline Singleton or the Module Namespace functional dissolution
Routes to the Kerievsky-Away or functional layer (either is accepted)
Frames the Singleton as unwarranted (nothing needs single-instance policy) and names the dissolution, rather than keeping or embellishing the pattern
Does not edit any source file and does not run tests (coach, don't drive)

evals.prompt: tests are green. the SAME `switch (shape.kind)` (circle / square / triangle) appears in three separate functions -- `area(shape)`, `perimeter(shape)`, and `boundingBox(shape)` -- so adding a new shape means editing all three switches. tests pass. whats the next refactoring?
evals.expected_output: Names Replace Conditional with Polymorphism (Fowler, NOT a Kerievsky pattern) for the same type switch repeated across sites; routed to the Fowler mechanical layer; does not edit code or run tests.
expectations: Names Replace Conditional with Polymorphism as the best-fit next refactoring
Routes to the Fowler mechanical layer (NOT a Kerievsky pattern-directed refactoring)
Justifies the Fowler mechanical route over a pattern-directed one: it is the SAME type switch repeated across sites, not a single complex conditional wanting a design pattern
Does not edit any source file and does not run tests (coach, don't drive)
